questions.question: Explain the difference between getDate() and getTimestamp() methods in JDBC with suitable examples.
keyPoints: getDate() returns only date portion (year, month, day) as java.sql.Date
getTimestamp() returns date + time + nanoseconds as java.sql.Timestamp
Use getDate() when only date matters (e.g., birth_date)
Use getTimestamp() for precise timing (e.g., transaction_time)
Timestamp truncates to database precision, Date truncates time component

questions.question: How does JDBC handle NULL values for primitive data types? What are the potential issues and solutions?
keyPoints: Primitive getters (getInt, getDouble) return 0/false for NULL - cannot distinguish from actual zero
wasNull() method must be called immediately after getter to check if last value was NULL
Alternative: use getObject() which returns Java null for SQL NULL
Wrapper classes (Integer, Double) can also be used with getObject() then unbox safely

questions.question: Describe the JDBC type mapping for DECIMAL/NUMERIC columns. Why is getBigDecimal() preferred over getDouble()?
keyPoints: DECIMAL maps to java.math.BigDecimal in Java
getDouble() uses IEEE 754 floating-point causing precision loss
BigDecimal stores exact decimal representation using BigInteger + scale
Example: 123.456 stored exactly in BigDecimal but approximated in double
Use getBigDecimal(precision, scale) for controlled precision in retrieval

questions.question: Write a code snippet to insert a BLOB (image) into a database using PreparedStatement.
keyPoints: Use setBinaryStream() for streaming large binary data
Alternatively use setBytes() for smaller blobs
Close streams properly after execution
Use try-with-resources for automatic cleanup
Example: pstmt.setBinaryStream(1, inputStream, fileLength)

questions.question: What is the purpose of java.sql.Types class in JDBC? List at least 5 type constants and their Java mappings.
keyPoints: Types class defines JDBC type constants matching SQL data types
VARCHAR → String (getString/setString)
INTEGER → int (getInt/setInt)
BIGINT → long (getLong/setLong)
DATE → java.sql.Date (getDate/setDate)
TIMESTAMP → java.sql.Timestamp (getTimestamp/setTimestamp)
BLOB → byte[] (getBytes/setBytes)

questions.question: How do you handle date values in JDBC when inserting data? Explain the conversion required from java.util.Date.
keyPoints: java.sql.Date extends java.util.Date but only stores date portion
Conversion required: new java.sql.Date(utilDate.getTime())
Time component in java.util.Date gets truncated in conversion
For time-only columns, use java.sql.Time
For timestamp, use new java.sql.Timestamp(utilDate.getTime())

questions.question: Explain the difference between setString(), setCharacterStream(), and setClob() for inserting large text data.
keyPoints: setString() - limited by driver for very large strings (usually 64KB limit)
setCharacterStream() - streams large text from Reader without loading entirely in memory
setClob() - accepts Clob object, provides methods for reading/writing large text
For very large CLOB, prefer setCharacterStream() for memory efficiency
setClob() may use temporary files depending on driver implementation

questions.question: Develop a complete JDBC program that demonstrates retrieval of different data types from a ResultSet, handling NULL values appropriately, and explaining the type conversion process.
keyPoints: Create Connection using DriverManager
Execute SELECT query returning various column types
Demonstrate getString(), getInt(), getDouble(), getDate(), getTimestamp(), getBigDecimal()
Show NULL handling with wasNull() check
Show proper date handling by converting between java.util.Date and java.sql.Date
Include try-catch for SQLException
Proper resource cleanup in finally block or try-with-resources

questions.question: Design a solution for storing and retrieving a large JSON document in a database using JDBC. Consider different approaches and their trade-offs.
keyPoints: Store as VARCHAR/TEXT - simple but limited size, use getString/setString
Store as CLOB - handles large text, use Clob API or CharacterStream
Store as Types.OTHER or database-specific JSON type - requires driver support
Retrieve using getString() for VARCHAR, getClob() or getCharacterStream() for CLOB
Consider: driver limitations, database JSON support, size requirements
Show PreparedStatement with setCharacterStream for insertion
Show retrieval with getClob().getCharacterStream() for large JSON

questions.question: Compare and contrast all numeric type mappings in JDBC. Create a decision guide for choosing the appropriate getter method based on data characteristics.
keyPoints: TINYINT → byte (getByte) - 8-bit signed integer
SMALLINT → short (getShort) - 16-bit signed
INTEGER/INT → int (getInt) - 32-bit signed
BIGINT → long (getLong) - 64-bit signed
REAL → float (getFloat) - 32-bit floating point
FLOAT/DOUBLE → double (getDouble) - 64-bit floating point
NUMERIC/DECIMAL → BigDecimal (getBigDecimal) - exact precision
Decision factors: size, precision needed, NULLability, performance
